data.title: Mitsubishi UFJ Financial: Eyes On Latest Investment And New Leader
data.description:
I'm still rating Mitsubishi UFJ (MUFG) (8306.T) as "Buy."

It made the right decision to invest in a "non banking financial institution/NBFI" called "Shriram Finance." I have a positive view of the incoming CEO.

My previous July 4, 2025, article touched on the potential turnaround of the bank's "Retail And Digital/RAD" operations and the improvement in its cash distributions.

Indian NBFI Is MUFG's New Associate Company

On Dec. 19 last year, the group revealed the purchase of "a 20% equity shares in Shriram Finance" for ₹0.4 trillion or $4.4 billion.

An Overview Of The Investee

I see the recent inorganic expansion as value-enhancing for multiple reasons.

"The RBI (Reserve Bank of India) has cut policy rates by 100 basis points" for the prior year, according to "FTSE Russell's" Dec. 9, 2025, piece. As per S&P Capital IQ, the consensus is anticipating another 0.25-0.50ppts (percentage points) reduction in '26.

Fund manager Wasatch is vested in two Indian "Non-Bank Financial Companies/NBFCs", namely "Cholamandalam Financial" and "Aavas Financier." Its thesis is that they "stand to gain from" RBI's actions, as "they have no deposit bases and must obtain their funding by borrowing from external sources."

Shriram will be a winner in India's changed monetary environment, just like Aavas/Cholamandalam. MUFG's mid-Dec '25 media release noted that the former is "the second largest retail NBFC in India."

The deal is also ROE-accretive based on my analysis.

Shriram's "Return On Equity/ROE" for 1HFY2025 (YE end-Mar '26) was 15.8%. That came in +230bps (basis points) above what MUFG registered during the same period.

Separately, this transaction allows MUFG to increase its exposure to a high-growth geographical region.

India's Potential

As per the chart presented above, the projected '22-37 NBFC-linked loan CAGR in Shriram's home market is a substantial +14%. Prior to this, MUFG's presence there was limited, with just six own-brand locations.

Also, there are opportunities for MUFG's various international businesses to work together.

The Japanese Bank's "Association of Southeast Asian Nations/ASEAN" Units

Shriram hosted an analyst call (S&P Capital IQ transcript) on December 30, 2025. It emphasized the acquirer's ASEAN entities can "learn from our experience here in India", and vice-versa.

MUFG's investees in other parts of Asia include e-wallet players "mynt/ascend" and e-commerce operators "Akulaku/Grab." They could help the Indian associate enter new digital-related business areas.

Looking ahead, the bank might raise its equity interest in Shriram.

At its own mid-December '25 briefing, MUFG indicated it "may explore various investment options." That was in response to a question on the "possibility of additional investment" for the affiliate company.

Executing Well On Leadership Succession

MUFG issued a 6-K filing on Dec 16, 2025, disclosing that "Junichi Hanzawa" will be the new CEO at the start of April this year. The outgoing company head "Hironori Kamezawa" will stay on the board, assuming the role of chairman.

My opinion of this transition is favorable.

Kamezawa isn't leaving the firm, which will ensure continuity to a large extent.

It always worries me when hired hands take over at the helm. They may have an insufficient understanding of the enterprise. The management style might also be a poor fit.

I'm glad to know Hanzawa is a long-tenured employee at the bank.

He has been with MUFG for almost four decades.

Hanzawa is now the president of the banking arm, a position he has held since 2Q2021. I believe he will place a strong emphasis on the "Retail And Digital/RAD" in the near future.

My early-Jul 2025 piece indicated that the "RAD segment's client acquisition metrics are way below what they were previously." I also pointed out that RAD's ROE was "inferior versus the group-level figure."

Hanzawa is overseeing critical RAD-related initiatives at MUFG Bank. These should garner greater attention when he becomes the CEO.

One of them is the launch of a new umbrella brand "M-tto" unifying its offerings in June '25. During the same month, online searches for "Mitsubishi UFJ" credit cards jumped "4x year-on-year" according to the July 22, 2025, "Investor Day" (S&P Capital IQ transcript). The re-branding exercise has delivered solid initial results.

MUFG's goal is to double yearly card issuance volume to 1 million by March 31, 2027. The main driver will be the planned roll-out of a company-wide loyalty scheme offering rewards called "M-tto points" in this calendar year.

At its "Investor Day", the firm highlighted that "customers who make 2 or more transactions have a 13x higher LTV (lifetime value) than customers who only have an account."

It's reasonable for me to infer that "M-tto" will be a major lever to revive the fortunes of RAD, given its function of stimulating cross-purchases. As the group's new leader, I think Hanzawa will make sure his key project is prioritized.

You Have To Consider These Risks

The management teams of MUFG and Shriram may find it difficult to get along due to cultural differences. In that scenario, the investment's actual synergies could be weaker than expected.

It's possible that the new CEO fails to secure the buy-in from his colleagues. The bank's growth might slow if projects are delayed or halted due to internal conflicts.

My Concluding Thoughts

I continue to be a bull on this name.

My updated "Gordon Growth Model/GGM"-derived P/B target is 1.64x. I changed the ROE input from 12.0% to 12.5%, reflecting the actual 1HFY25 performance.

MUFG's capital appreciation potential is +21% (1.64/1.35), given its current PB of 1.35 times. Shriram's investment and Hanzawa's appointment justify a re-rating.

data.title: XAR: What If The Biggest Increase In Military Budgets Since The Cold War Is Just Beginning?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (NYSEARCA:XAR). This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on July 3, 2025 with a buy recommendation. My intention in this article is to analyze recent developments in the thesis, especially the possible increase in the American military budget, and how this could impact the companies in the XAR ETF.

State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF

The XAR ETF tracks the S&P® Aerospace & Defense Select Industry® Index. It is a simple way for investors who want exposure to the U.S. Aerospace & Defense Industry. In one year, while the benchmark index increased by 59.87% YoY, XAR ETF increased 59.83% YoY, therefore, the tracking error is very low.

The ETF has 41 holdings, and the main three have a 12.68% share, so there is a relevant level of diversification. Below are the main holdings of this ETF:

Among the main pairs, the XAR ETF stands out with the best expense ratio (0.35%) and the third largest amount of AUM ($5.34 billion). After this introduction, let's revisit the thesis.

Context

Recently, a small window of opportunity emerged when a speech by President Trump caused the shares of these companies to fall. At the time, the president stated that he will block buybacks or dividends in defense companies until they meet certain criteria. He also criticized the high salaries of executives at these companies.

For pragmatic investors, the event above was a great opportunity to buy these assets at more attractive prices, as the main catalysts for the thesis remain intact. The great example of this is that President Trump proposed increasing the defense budget from $1 trillion to $1.5 trillion, and that in itself would be a reason for me to reiterate my recommendation to buy the XAR ETF, but it is necessary for the investor to understand what is behind this.

My Review

The scenario remains promising for these assets because, according to the American national security strategy, geopolitics has become vital to the defense of United States interests. Although the objective here is to discuss investments, we need to briefly understand the cause of the problem, to then understand the consequence, which is the tailwind for aerospace and defense assets.

A great example was the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro. Through this event, the United States made clear its intention to reduce the influence of Russia, and mainly China, on the American continent. Venezuela combined influences from Russia, China, and even regimes like Iran, and reducing these influences is strategic for the US in accordance with its national security strategy. The fact is that to reduce this influence, it is necessary to invest in military power, which boosts related assets.

Another example is Greenland. According to the principles of the national security strategy, it is extremely strategic for the United States to possess the island. Greenland is the largest island in the world, and melting Arctic ice will create new shipping routes.

While I'm not expressing my opinion on this, but I believe the most important thing from an American strategic point of view is to increase the shield against Russia and China. Again, this is the cause, and the consequence is more investments in related assets.

Therefore, the main catalyst for the XAR ETF, in my opinion, is the American national security strategy. It is clear that this strategy was not created overnight; it is the result of a series of events that have occurred over the last few decades. But we are not here to discuss that but rather to discuss the effects of it, and the effects are a boost to assets in the defense and aerospace sector. But as investors, we need to understand whether the price of these promising assets is expensive or cheap.

Valuation

Initially, we will carry out a temporal analysis of the P/E multiple of the XAR ETF and draw some conclusions.

As I said, prices are at 52-week highs, and the P/E multiple is at 52-week highs as well. However, this does not prevent me from reiterating my recommendation to buy. I affirm this by analyzing the American military budget in relation to GDP.

Although a scenario of increased geopolitical risks is emerging, the American military budget in relation to GDP is still very low. Of course this involves broader discussions such as the fiscal situation, however, it is very likely that the military budget will actually increase, and I would not rule out an increase in the future beyond the $1.5 trillion suggested by President Trump. Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy the XAR ETF. Additionally, I feel very confident in the recommendation as SA Quant tools also recommend buying.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

I remain convinced that the main risk to this thesis involves reallocations in military budgets. But how could this happen? Well, the most possible way, in my opinion, would be for one of these countries to enter an aggressive recession.

Let's imagine that Russia's economy collapses if oil prices fall to even lower prices, or let's imagine that the Chinese economy plunges into a deep recession due to the more severe impacts of the housing crisis.

Such recessions could trigger reallocations in military budgets, and this could have a ripple effect on global military budgets, as risks would be reduced, in theory.

The Bottom Line

The year 2026 began with a major geopolitical event in the Americas: the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro. This is the symptom, not the cause, of a series of events that cause countries to increase their military budgets, and consequently, ETFs like XAR benefit.

Therefore, despite the strong rise in ETF prices since my initial coverage thesis, I identify that the thesis remains completely preserved, and going further, I would not doubt even greater increases in military budgets in the future, which would further boost the prices of related companies.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy the XAR ETF. The investment thesis is one of the hottest and most promising of 2026 and the risk-return ratio remains very attractive in my opinion.

data.title: WD-40 Company's Drop Doesn't Justify An Upgrade
data.description:
Even though I don't write about it as much as I would like to, one of my favorite companies in the world is WD-40 Company (WDFC). I don't mean this from an investment perspective. Rather, I mean it from a business model perspective. It is conceptually a simple firm that has established for itself a significant market presence. Even though it is not a high growth play by any means, it has established a track record of consistent growth. That, combined with a solid balance sheet, has afforded the company attractive trading multiples.

But lately, things have not been going according to plan. Even though revenue continues to grow, profitability has become a bit of an issue. And sadly, the market has punished the stock because of this. Shares are actually down 18.3% since I last reaffirmed it as a Hold candidate back in January of 2024. Since then, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up an astounding 45.4%.

The most recent data provided by a management covers the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. Those results came out after the market closed on January 8th. Shares of the business fell nearly 9% in after hours trading in response to this news. When it comes to this year in its entirety, the company expects attractive top line performance. But profitability will almost certainly be weaker because of certain headwinds that the business is dealing with. Given how expensive the stock is on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, downgrading it was tempting. But because of the extra wiggle room I afford these opportunities, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold is acceptable.

A though quarter

After the market closed on January 8th, the management team at WD-40 Company announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $154.4 million. Although that was up from the $153.5 million the company reported a year earlier, the increase fell short of what analysts expected to the tune of $0.7 million. The biggest hit that the company saw involved its HCCP category of products, which involves its home care and cleaning products. Overall revenue there plummeted from $8 million to $5.5 million. According to management, this drop in revenue was the result of distribution changes that the company had made, as well as lower demand in the U.S. that the company blamed on a reduction in advertising and promotional activities. It is important to note that these sales also dropped in the EIMEA (Europe, India, Middle East, and Africa) regions declining from $1.6 million to nothing. But this part was intentional, with the company specifically selling off its home care and cleaning product brands in that region in the final quarter of 2025.

This was not the only area of weakness that we saw. In fact, the WD-40 Multi-Use Product category also saw a decline. Revenue dropped from $118.5 million to $117.8 million. This drop in revenue came in spite of the fact that, in the Americas, sales grew from $52.9 million to $54.6 million because of a rise in volumes as the company continues to expand its distribution activities and saw success from its promotional activities. Higher pricing in Mexico also helped here. The real pain point for this part of the company involved its Asia Pacific region, with revenue dropping sharply from $20.8 million to $18.3 million.

This plunge of 12.1% can be chalked up to changes in timing of customer orders as, according to management, distributors that had previously participated significantly and the firm's promotional activities in the final quarter of 2025 decided to adjust to more normal levels of inventory in the first quarter of this year. China is a pretty significant market for the company. And going into this, I thought that perhaps that was the weak spot. But it was actually the parts of Asia outside of China that proved to be problematic. There is some good news here, however. Management said that they are seeing their Asian customers coming in with stronger demand. And they expect that to continue moving forward.

The real area of growth for the company ended up being its WD-40 Specialist operations, with revenue shooting up from $19.2 million to $22.5 million. In the Americas, the growth was driven by a rise in online retail sales, new distribution investments finally paying off, and greater demand in the U.S. The EIMEA was even more impressive, with revenue jumping around 27%, with France and Iberia leading the way. The company saw strong volume growth in those markets as it stepped up its promotional activities and rolled out new products in the first quarter of the year.

Normally, you would expect revenue growth to translate to higher profitability. But that's not what we saw here. Earnings per share dropped from $1.39 to $1.28, taking net income down from $18.9 million $17.5 million. Looking at the data closely, we can see that this drop was the result of a surge in selling, general, and administrative costs from $50.5 million to $55.3 million. Employee related expenses increased because of annual compensation increases, as well as a rise in head count. This might seem as though the cost structure of the business is out of control. However, the company said that this growth can be chalked up to deliberate moves that the business is making aimed at supporting sales growth initiatives. There were also higher travel and meeting expenses associated with these types of growth oriented investments. I would like to point out that the earnings per share reported by the company ended up missing analysts’ expectations by $0.17.

Other profitability metrics for the business worsened also. Operating cash flow declined from $14.9 million to $10 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $24.3 million to $22.4 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $27.5 million to $25.6 million. This drop that the company experienced during the start of the 2026 fiscal year marks something of a change for the company. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the last three completed fiscal years. With the exception of operating cash flow, which has declined, all of the other metrics shown in that chart have improved. This is a testament to the strength and stability of the business.

When it comes to 2026, management said that investors should expect revenue of between $630 million and $655 million. Even at the low end, that would be above the $620 million the company reported for 2025. But unfortunately, the earnings per share guidance of between $5.75 and $6.15 implies net profits, at the midpoint, of $79.7 million. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2026, we would also expect adjusted operating cash flow to drop to $90.7 million while EBITDA would contract to $106.5 million.

This picture for the company is not ideal. But I wouldn't call it all for either. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued using these estimates for 2026, as well as historical results for the last two completed fiscal years. These are certainly expensive multiples. And as the chart below illustrates, WD-40 Company is more expensive than other companies that have some similarities to it:

  • Energizer Holdings, Inc. (ENR)
  • Spectrum Brands Holdings, Inc. (SPB)
  • Central Garden & Pet Company (CENT)
  • Reynolds Consumer Products Inc. (REYN)
  • The Clorox Company (CLX).

But to be clear, these are not exactly perfect comparables. They are, instead, world famous brands that have a history themselves of performing well.

Even with the additional flexibility that I afford high quality companies such as WD-40 Company, these multiples are difficult to justify. Having said that, there is a reason that I am more neutral than I am anything else. And this relates to the overall strategy that the company is working on. Management has specifically pointed out that there are a couple of good growth opportunities here. In the WD-40 Multi-Use Product space, the firm believes that the overall revenue opportunity for the company is around $1.9 billion.

That implies growth potential of $1.4 billion. The biggest growth prospects here include some of the most predictable countries, such as China, India, Japan, and Indonesia. But even smaller nations like Germany, South Korea, Saudi Arabia, Italy, Norway, and others, are being targeted by the business. This is not the only area of growth that the company has noticed.

There's also the WD-40 Specialist category, with the firm pegging that market opportunity at $665 million. That would imply long term growth for the business of $583 million. That, truly, would represent significant upside potential. And it deserves our attention.

Takeaway

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I would say that the picture is looking mixed for WD-40 Company. However, I am comforted by the fact that the company believes that recent challenges are transitory and that the picture is already improving as of the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. Bottom line results have certainly been disappointing as well. But with management focusing their efforts on a few key areas that could add even more value as time goes on, I'm not concerned yet. I do think, however, that the company deserves more of a Hold rating than it does anything else right now. And if the stock rises much from where it is, downgrading it to a Sell would not be unreasonable.

data.title: Eagle Bancorp Aims To Return To Profitability
data.description:
On January 21, Eagle Bancorp (EGBN) will report its Q4 financial results for 2025. After reporting back-to-back quarters of significant losses, EGBN is in desperate need of some positive momentum. The company has been struggling with loan quality and slashed its quarterly dividend by 94% to just $0.01 in Q3 2025. While there are signs that the worst may be over, there is still a great deal of uncertainty around the company and its operations. EGBN is expected to report a loss of $0.01 in Q4 2025, and downward revisions for the company’s bottom line for the full year have outnumbered improvements three to one.

Much of what I uncovered in my research into the company led me to believe that this is a stock to sell. Descending profits for the company actually started back in early 2022. Company revenue has now fallen for the last six quarters leading into Q4 for this year. However, EGBN had a book value of $37 at the end of Q3, which means that it is currently trading at a discount of over 40% to that number. Given a few other glimmers of hope for the year ahead, there is just enough meat on the bone for me to keep Eagle Bancorp in the Hold category leading up to the Q4 report that is due in two weeks.

Overview of Eagle Bancorp

Eagle Bancorp is the holding company for EagleBank, operating 12 bank branches and four lending centers in Northern Virginia, Suburban Maryland, and the District of Columbia. The main business activities of EagleBank include real estate and commercial lending as well as traditional consumer banking services. EagleBank has a relatively short history that dates back to 1998. The company also owns Landroval Municipal Finance, a subsidiary that focuses on lending to municipalities by buying debt on the public market.

A snapshot of the company’s financial position at the end of Q3 2025 is shown below. The share price for EGBN has increased 12.8% since that report was released, giving the company a current market cap of $635 million. Eagle Bancorp is a member of both the Russell 2000 and the S&P Small Cap 600 Indices.

Additional details about the bank’s loan portfolio are given below. Total loans outstanding from the bank have decreased by almost 8.4% since Q3 2024. The company has been making an effort to prioritize credit quality and sell challenged office loans to reduce exposure in that area. Office CRE represented 11% of the portfolio as recently as Q2 2025. Its share is now 8%. The majority of the remaining loans from EagleBank are still CRE of some type. Commercial and industrial loans actually increased in Q3 by $105 million and now comprise 17% of the loan mix.

At the end of its most recently reported quarter, Eagle Bancorp owned a portfolio of investment securities worth almost $1.95 billion and deposits with other banks totaling $841 million. The bank is still well above minimum thresholds for capital and has been able to maintain a common equity tier 1 ratio above many of its peers. Its tangible common equity to tangible assets ratio of 10.4% currently places it fifth out of 18 other similar banks.

Operations Analysis

While the company has reported significant losses this year, its profitability issues date back to FY 2022. From FY 2021 to FY 2023, normalized earnings per share for Eagle Bancorp decreased 43.5%. In Q2 of 2024, EGBN recorded a goodwill impairment charge of $104.2 million. In large part because of this adjustment, normalized earnings fell again in FY 2024 by 41.8%. This year, the company has found a way to make matters even worse, with a loss of $2.30 per share in Q2 and $2.22 per share in Q3. Total shareholders’ equity has declined 8.3% since Q3 2024 and 5.2% from Q2 2025.

Most other performance metrics from EGBN have been impacted by the charges and adjustments that the company has taken to shore up its loan portfolio in 2025. To be fair, there were warning signs in earlier quarters, as Eagle Bancorp’s return on assets was below 0.5% in Q4 2024 and Q1 2025 before things really took a turn south. Its return on average tangible common equity was also dragging late last year before hitting what the company hopes will be rock bottom in Q2 and Q3 of 2025. The bank’s net interest margin is also well below average, having been under 2.5% for the last five reported quarters. It stood at 2.43% at the end of Q3, six basis points better than Q2.

Non-interest income fell $3.9 million in Q3 2025, from $6.4 million in the previous quarter to just $2.9 million. This was largely due to a $3.6 million loss that the bank incurred on the sale of two loans and a $2.0 million loss on the sale of a set of investment securities to reposition its investment portfolio and reduce the bank’s exposure to higher-cost brokered funding. It is simply difficult to find much of a silver lining to the bank's operations from the last 24 months.

Troubled Loan Portfolio

The biggest issue for Eagle Bancorp in 2025 has been a complete washout of the quality of its loan portfolio, with commercial real estate being the largest culprit. During the third quarter, the company contracted with a loan review firm to conduct an assessment of its commercial real estate and commercial and industrial loan portfolios. A snapshot of the bank’s asset quality metrics is shown below.

The company’s allowance for credit losses was 2.14% at the end of Q3, down 24 basis points from Q2. (If not for EGBN being able to book a $24.9 million credit for provisional credit losses in Q3, its reported net income would have been even lower.) Non-performing assets decreased by $95.5 million in Q3, which represented 1.23% of total assets. That was an improvement of 93 basis points from the second quarter.

Of the $7.3 billion in loans controlled by EagleBank at the end of Q3, a staggering $958.5 million were considered to be substandard or special mention. This was actually an increase from the end of Q2, when that number stood at $875.4 million. For a bank with similar assets, the average ratio of net charge-offs to average loans is 0.59%. For EagleBank, that number is almost 12.5 times that level at 7.36%, an increase of 3.14% from Q2.

Additional Risks to Consider

As if these issues discussed above are not enough for EGBN shareholders to worry about, there are a couple of other risks to consider as the bank moves into the new year. For the last several quarters, the company has opened its earnings presentation slide deck with a map of its branch locations in the DC metro area, referring to its “attractive Washington DC footprint.” It references the household income for the area, which is above the national average. It also talks about how the local economy is well supported by Federal government spending. However, Q4 2025 was not a great time to be dependent on government jobs, as the country experienced a 43-day government shutdown. A new funding deadline will arrive at the end of this month, and there are no guarantees that funding bills will be passed in time to avoid another one. Even if a new agreement is reached, government spending and job opportunities may come under additional pressure moving forward. The District of Columbia, Maryland, and Virginia were three of the top six most impacted states/regions during the last shutdown because of their dependence on government activity.

Even though I use EGBN’s tangible book value as a support for holding on to the stock, there are no guarantees that the value will ever be realized by shareholders. In October, First Foundation (FFWM) announced a deal to be acquired by FirstSun Capital Bancorp (FSUN). FFWM is a regional bank with headquarters in Texas that I wrote about last August. Like EGBN, First Foundation had some recent issues with problem loans. Its value in the takeover by FSUN was only 0.8x TBV. In December, OceanFirst Financial (OCFC) agreed to purchase Flushing Financial (FFIC) for the same TBV ratio. FFIC has also recently dealt with faltering asset quality and declining loan balances. In short, a buyout that could help dig Eagle Bancorp out of its hole may not command much of a premium above its current share price.

Conclusion

As part of its Q3 2025 earnings presentation, Eagle Bancorp detailed its outlook for FY 2026, which painted a mixed picture for its future. The company expects deposits to slide downward 1 to 4%, but some of that decline is attributed to its decision to solicit fewer brokered deposits next year. Average loans for the bank are expected to decrease by 1 to 3%. Average earning assets for Eagle Bancorp will drop 5 to 7%.

However, net interest margin is projected to rise by 15 to 20 basis points, and non-interest income is expected to grow 20 to 30% after expanding 27 to 32% in FY 2025. The company is also expected to benefit from a lower tax rate that should decline from a range of 27 to 32% this year to just 15 to 20% in FY 2026. The actions taken by the bank to clean up its balance sheet in 2025 should lead to a smoother experience for shareholders next year, but it will be a slow road back to the profit levels that it experienced just two or three years ago.

A profitable Q4 and a sign that the bank’s scrubbing of its loan portfolio is finally complete would make me feel significantly better about its future. The bank has enough liquidity to weather the current storm in the short term, assuming there are no others looming around the corner. Analysts expect the company to return to profitability in FY 2026 with an EPS estimate of $1.57.

The bank did mention in its last conference call that a few more charges could be coming in its Q4 2025 report, as it was attempting to sell an additional $121 million of its loans that were in different stages of disposition. Investors may have to wait until the first financial report from FY 2026 to get a better idea of just what the future will bring. Eagle Bancorp is a Hold, at best, until there are meaningful signs of a turnaround in the new year.

data.title: Trillion Dollar Market Cap Changing Of The Guard
data.description:
For the past several months, NVIDIA (NVDA) has held the title of the world's largest stock by market cap. While NVDA has pulled back from a peak market cap just above $5 trillion in late October/early November to $4.5 trillion today, there's still a significant gap versus the next largest stock: Alphabet (GOOGL).

GOOGL in the #2 spot is a new development. As shown below, it has typically been either Apple (AAPL) or Microsoft (MSFT) holding that position in recent years; however, both of those stocks have pulled back as GOOGL has pressed to new highs. As shown in the second chart below, this is the first time that AAPL is smaller than GOOGL since January 29, 2019. Additionally, we would note that AAPL is currently larger than Microsoft, but that gap has also been narrowing. In the past several years, the two stocks have repeatedly gone back and forth for the larger market cap of the two.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Neogen: Starting 2026 With A Bang
data.description:
In October I concluded that Neogen (NEOG), itself being a food safety play, was still not a safety play itself, at least for investors. Following a well-documented and incredibly value-destructive 3M Food Safety acquisition/merger, the business has suffered from a weak performance and high leverage for years.

A recent divestment was welcomed to tackle debt, and while cost-cutting moves were announced, profits were elusive. Having initiated a long position before, I noticed the potential but failed to see sufficient green shots to add to that position. By now green shoots have arrived, triggering a massive move higher in the shares on the back of relatively modest numerical improvements, but the direction of travel changed, and that is key now.

Anything But A Safety Play

Neogen was founded in the 1980s with a goal to create a bright future for global food security, with people and animals having access to a food supply that is safe, sufficient, and sustainable. Providing such services includes producing products and services like indicator testing, pathogens, hygiene monitoring, animal care, veterinary instruments, etc. These revenues are split pretty evenly between North America and the rest of the world.

Many of these positives are so-called consumables, often relating to testing, with relatively low cost of tests but a critical need to perform such tasks, which in theory creates a very defensive positioning.

Just a $5 stock in 2010, the company was a real investor darling in the 2010s as shares rose to levels in the $40s later in the decade as the company delivered on solid growth and margins. Over the past decade, the company grew sales from $300 million in 2015 to $500 million in 2022, with GAAP operating margins reported well in the teens.

That thesis changed overnight as the company announced a huge $5 billion deal to acquire 3M Food Safety, a deal designed to double the sales base to $1 billion. This all took place after management loaded up with quite some confidence, this being a $50 stock in 2021.

Just Disappointments

The pro forma billion-dollar business, upon the deal announcement, ended up posting sales of just $924 million in the fiscal year 2024. Worse, EBITDA of $213 million fell short of a $300 million promised number, and there was the issue with net debt reported exceeding $700 million.

Even worse, the company guided for 2025 sales to fall just below $900 million, with EBITDA seen just below $200 million, as a $50 stock was down to $5 in April of last year, for a $1.9 billion enterprise valuation. This was very painful, as the company paid $5 billion for just part of the business just a couple of years before! However, the company already reduced its footprint, announcing a $130 million deal to sell its Cleaners & Disinfectants business, bringing in some much-needed cash to address leverage.

That move, taking place at acceptable revenue multiples, would help to reduce leverage ratios a bit, but over the fall, shares were stuck around $6 per share. 2025 sales were down another 3% to $895 million, with EBITDA reported at just $184 million, hardly inspiring. The real issue is that sales were seen down to a midpoint of $830 million in 2026, with EBITDA seen down to $170 million, as some of these declines could be attributed to the sale of the Cleaners & Disinfectants business, but not all.

In October, the company reported a 4% fall in first-quarter sales to $209 million, as net debt was down to $661 million, for a 3.6 times leverage ratio based on the EBITDA outlook. The issue is that while EBITDA numbers were posted, adjusted earnings came in at a paltry four cents, these furthermore being highly adjusted numbers, this limiting my enthusiasm to add to a modest long position here.

Starting 2026 On A Strong Note

After making two leadership appointments in the first week of trading, the company reported encouraging second-quarter results. While reported second-quarter sales were down 3% to $225 million, core sales growth, so excluding the divestment, was up nearly 3%. Adjusted earnings were reported at $0.10 per share, down a penny on the year before but up six cents from the first quarter!

This profit number is equal to about $22 million in dollar terms, still standing in sharp contrast to a near $16 million GAAP loss, that being largely due to amortization charges. Other charges are quite frequent, often involve cash payments, and not all adjustments can be accepted on management's blue eyes, including, of course, modest stock-based compensation expenses.

The big game changer is that after years of revenue declines, the company upped the full-year guidance and now sees sales around $850 million. This marks a $20 million increase from the previous guidance. It appears that finally some momentum has arrived for the business, driven by hard internal work.

EBITDA is now seen around $175 million, a $5 million improvement on that front compared to the previous guidance. As net debt is reported at $655 million, leverage remains elevated at 3.7 times, but the direction of travel is positive, that is for sure, as the company furthermore confirmed on the call that it is exploring alternatives for its genomics business.

Updating The Take

The 271 million shares outstanding of Neogen popped from nearly $7.50 to nearly $10 per share upon the news announcement, adding half a billion in value on the back of the positive story. By now the market value has jumped to $2.1 billion, for a near $2.8 billion enterprise valuation.

At this point, the enterprise valuation has risen to over 3 times sales over 15 times EBITDA, with realistic earnings still not very noteworthy, as investors for now are relieved that the worst is over. If the company can return to posting realistic operating profits of 15%-20% on a $900 million revenue base, operating profits of $135-$180 million might result in net earnings between $75 million and $110 million, equal to about $0.35-$0.50 per share.

This means that some good news has been priced in, and while the company commanded much higher multiples in the 2010s, a lot of the track record has been wiped out recently.

Frankly, the re-rating is quite substantial, with shares up two-thirds since October, following a monstrous rally in just three months. However, the pace of improvements is dramatic, with EBITDA margins up 5 points in just a quarter!

Weighing all this, I remain constructive but more cautious on the shares, just as the green shoots have come through, causing shares to have been repriced quite aggressively. For now I will "Hold" on to the shares, but if shares might really rally to the lower teens, I am definitely considering trimming some of the position.

data.title: Greenbrier Companies: Run With House Money
data.description:
We last issued a Buy call on The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) stock in April of 2025. Shares have rallied some 30% from there. In past trades, we have consistently maintained that when you buy in the 20s and 30s, moving to a Hold rating makes sense when you get into the $50s. Given the price action we have seen, we do believe moving to a hold makes sense here at about $52 a share. We think if you took our trade, then running a house position is a good idea given the buybacks and dividends. By a house position, we mean taking an approach of selling out your initial investment plus 25%-50% of the profit and letting the rest run long-term as an investment. We teach this strategy to our investing group members at BAD BEAT Investing with the goal of laying the groundwork for long-term wealth creation.

GBX makes a good candidate here. By taking this approach, you will have exposure to all future gains in the stock but, more importantly, access to the dividends, any spinoffs, and benefits of share repurchases. With the principal and a chunk of the profit now in cash, we then move on to better near-term opportunities for gains like you will have locked in with GBX. That said, we like the operations and momentum here, and today, we check back in on GBX. Let us discuss fiscal Q1 results and the outlook for the year ahead.

Greenbrier Companies Fiscal Q1 Headline Earnings Results

In the just-reported fiscal quarter, the top-line revenue figure was actually ahead of expectations for revenue. Q1 revenues were down some 20% from last year to $706 million. This was driven by lower deliveries and fewer new railcar orders. While the lease fleet has grown, clearly looking at the trend of the last few quarters, there is some pressure. The backlog is still healthy, and despite lower revenue, the margins and core EBITDA reflect solid expense management. We saw strong earnings on the bottom line relative to expectations. The adjusted EPS of $1.14 surpassed our expectations of $0.90-$1.00 in earnings per share and beat the Street consensus by $0.35.

Lorie Tekorius, CEO and President, stated in the earnings press release:

Greenbrier delivered solid results in Q1. Leasing and Fleet Management provided stability through strong execution and recurring cash flows including selectively recycling capital through fleet sales in a strong equipment market to support liquidity and balance sheet strength. Manufacturing achieved good operating performance on lower volumes"

While operational performance was a bit better than expected, gross margin overall was 14.6% in Q1, down 430 basis points from the sequential quarter. The operating margin of 8.7% was also down 80 basis points. Lower production and maintenance volumes impacted margins.

Segment Summary

Greenbrier is now a manufacturing and fleet management segment. Both segments were a bit lighter than the sequential Q4 as expected, though overall performance was better than expected. Gross margin was 11.0%, down from 15.7% in the manufacturing segment in the linked quarter. This was a result of operating inefficiencies in production rates and throughput in new railcar facilities. There were also fewer deliveries, as was expected and planned. Operating margin was down to 7.4%, versus 10.8% in the sequential Q4.

In the leasing and fleet management services segment, gross margins slipped due to less fleet income. Gross margin was 63.5% versus 64.9% in the sequential Q4. However, operating margin benefited from a bounce in equipment sales and hit 89.6%, up dramatically from 53.0% in Q4. While such a margin cannot be sustained, it helped drive earnings from operations in this segment higher and led to a better-than-expected earnings result, as we noted above.

Order Update

New railcar orders in fiscal Q1 totaled 3,700 units, and these were valued at $550 million. Deliveries were lower than expected at just 4,400 in Q1, and the company grew the lease fleet by another 1,200 units to 16,700 units. The backlog remains strong, though it has come down in recent years. Examining the backlog helps project some revenue and cash flow over the next year. In Q1, the new railcar backlog was 16,300, valued at $2.2 billion. The backlog has for the most part been in the 20,000s on average over time, so this is well below the historical average.

Dividends And Repurchases

Holding a house position for the long term is a good approach here given the dividends and repurchases. The Board just declared a quarterly dividend of $0.32 per share, payable on February 17, 2026, to shareholders of record as of January 27, 2026. This is Greenbrier's 47th consecutive quarterly dividend and is $0.02 higher than the quarterly dividend from when we last covered the company. Management also bought back 303,000 shares in the quarter for $13 million and has $65 million remaining in the authorization.

Forward View

Looking ahead, the company expects 17,500-20,500 deliveries of railcars for the year. This is, of course, down from historical averages. Revenue should come in between $2.7 billion and $3.2 billion, while capex will be down to $285 million, while the company plans to sell another $165 million of its equipment this year. Assuming we see margins in the mid- to high-teens for the year, we see EPS coming in between $3.85 and $5.00. So, EPS trends are lower, though value persists. We like holding a house position.

data.title: SMX Has Promising Traceability Tech, But Hold For Now
data.description:
SMX (Security Matters) Public Limited Company (SMX) is a company that produces invisible molecular markers. These are used in materials to provide them with a traceable identity that can be linked to a digital record that can be hosted on a device, on private servers, on the cloud, or in a blockchain ledger. This identity supports control across the lifecycle of products, including recycling. Recently, the company extended its circular-rubber program into latex and rubber gloves, in a pilot stage initiative. But, overall, I don’t see enough tangible value to justify a bullish rating at this stage, which is why I rate SMX a “Hold” for now.

Molecular Markers

SMX (Security Matters) Public Limited Company is a technology company that delivers brand protection, authentication, and track-and-traced products for businesses. SMX was incorporated in 2022, initially as Empatan plc. The company is the product of a de-SPAC merger between Lionheart III Corp. (LION), which was the SPAC company, and Security Matters, which previously was traded on the Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) as Security Matters Limited. The merger closed in March 2023, and SMX is also listed in Dublin, Ireland.

As a quick overview, SMX offers an identification system with three main components: markers, readers with a proprietary algorithm, and a digital platform. The markers are physical and chemical. They are made of embedded sub-molecular particles that can be applied to solids, liquids, or gases in a permanent or removable fashion. SMX’s Markers are unique, hard to duplicate, and they are verifiable even after industrial processes where other labels are lost. Designated readers detect the markers, and the algorithm identifies and validates the marked material or product.

Ultimately, SMX’s digital platform or ledger captures the data for the product and stores it locally on the readers' private servers, on cloud infrastructure, or on a blockchain digital ledger to preserve integrity and custody. This is how SMX’s products solve authentication and anti-counterfeit problems. Also, these products give materials memory to monitor all the events in the supply chain to enhance its integrity, quality assurance, and brand protection. Additionally, SMX’s offerings allow recycling and plastics identification, detecting the markers for better sorting and higher-quality recyclate.

Safer Circular Recovery

Moreover, SMX announced that they’re expanding their industrial rubber traceability platform into the latex and rubber gloves market. SMX called it the sixth application of its rubber program, aiming to make gloves traceable at the material level to facilitate recovery and recycling. Glove recycling is difficult because they often have contamination risks from biological or chemical factors.

This matters because gloves are not easy to classify due to the difficulty in distinguishing in a reliable way and at scale among latex, nitrile, neoprene, or blends. As a result, most end up in landfills or incineration. SMX’s system delivers memory to the materials that persists across its use and processing, maintaining digital records to allow classification and the recovery of materials when it is safe. With that in mind, note that the glove industry has high product volumes, with a market estimated at $13.8 billion in 2024. But this TAM could exceed $21.6 billion by 2030, with annual consumption exceeding 330 billion units.

Therefore, this type of product volume justifies that even small improvements in recovery could translate into meaningful gains. However, according to the press release, this initiative is still a pilot and the building of an ecosystem, not yet contracts with revenue. Similarly, previous applications of SMX’s circular-rubber program include bicycle tires, engine mounts, internal rubber components, vehicle tires, and truck tires. Most of these initiatives are still in pilots, trials, and validation runs, with no clear evidence of full commercial-scale rollouts.

Interestingly, the EU’s Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) introduces the Digital Product Passport (DPP) as a digital record to identify products, with product-specific requirements progressively with fuller rollout toward 2030. I believe this new regulation could become a tailwind for SMX because it overlaps with its focus on materials traceability and circularity. Time will tell if SMX capitalizes on this type of regulatory initiative going forward.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

In any event, from a valuation perspective, SMX now trades at a $295.5 million market cap. The latest information we have is from their unaudited financials as of June 2025, which show their balance sheet holds $750.0 thousand in cash against approximately $7.7 million in financial debt (loans and convertible notes), aside from other regular operating liabilities. However, the majority of SMX’s assets are in goodwill ($26.1 million) and intangibles ($10.5 million). That’s how SMX’s book value reached $38.9 million, which would imply a high P/B of 7.6 using those figures. For comparison, the sector’s median P/B is much lower at around 3.7.

Moreover, the same proforma financials (June 2025) show SMX burned through approximately $4.2 million during 1H2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX. This would suggest a very limited cash runway at the time. That’s why SMX had to do its recent equity raises. By December 2025, SMX disclosed that it’s going to raise $16.5 million through convertible promissory notes. So, adding that to their 1H2025 cash balances, it would imply a cash runway of approximately 2.1 years, which is much more manageable.

Additionally, we know SMX secured a $100 million equity line that functions similar to an ATM. So, in total, SMX could access up to $116.5 million in additional capital if needed. However, I think the best approach is to simply work with the recent $16.5 million in promissory notes. That would imply a total debt of roughly $24.2 million and $17.3 million in cash. Thus, I estimate SMX’s EV at around $302.4 million, which would still place it decisively in highly speculative microcap territory.

But, despite being a relatively tiny company, the stock does have a very healthy average trading volume of 2.5 million shares. At the current PPS of $28.00, that implies reasonably good liquidity of $70.0 million for a microcap. In that sense, I would say this name will likely have manageable slippage costs and volatility relative to most microcaps, which is a plus that does derisk its investment profile to some extent.

Conclusion: Not Worth The Risk For Now

Overall, I feel SMX remains in pilot stages with a promising tracking technology that hasn’t found its product-market fit yet. Unfortunately, SMX doesn’t have any meaningful revenues today to justify its current valuation. And I believe its current market cap is mostly just pricing in the optionality from its sizeable TAM and recent capital infusion. But, from an investment angle, I don’t see any meaningful revenue milestone in the near term that could warrant a bullish rating. Similarly, I think SMX probably has enough access to capital to work on its vision, which could pay off eventually if it succeeds. Thus, on balance, I lean towards a “Hold” rating for now.

data.title: Why Venezuela's Political Transition Has Left Oil Markets Largely Unmoved
data.description:
By Łukasz Zembik

Global oil prices have reacted only marginally to Venezuela’s political transition, reflecting a market consensus that the country will not deliver a meaningful increase in crude supply in the near future. While Venezuela holds vast oil reserves, investors remain focused on practical constraints - limited production capacity, deteriorated infrastructure, and weak investment economics - which prevent any rapid supply response.

Production capacity remains severely limited

At present, Venezuela’s effective production capacity is estimated at around 1 million barrels per day, only slightly above current output and far below the roughly 2.5 million barrels per day produced a decade ago. Years of mismanagement, underinvestment, and international sanctions have left the oil sector unable to scale up quickly. Recent U.S. actions have reinforced these limits: sanctions on shipping reduced exports to about 500,000 barrels per day, forcing production cuts as storage filled up. Even proposals for the U.S. to purchase oil held in floating storage would ease logistical pressures rather than unlock new supply.

Weak investment economics and high costs

Some modest recovery is possible if sanctions are eased and the United States becomes a stable buyer. Smaller, risk-tolerant producers could restart marginal fields, potentially lifting output by around 300,000 barrels per day over the next two to three years. This would bring production toward roughly 1.4 million barrels per day - still insufficient to influence a global market consuming more than 100 million barrels per day.

Structural and economic barriers remain substantial. Venezuela’s heavy, high-sulfur crude requires costly extraction, blending, and specialized refining, resulting in persistent discounts to benchmarks such as WTI. Estimates suggest breakeven prices could approach USD 80 per barrel, making large-scale investment unattractive at current price levels. Rebuilding the sector would require around USD 100 billion in total investment, with annual spending of roughly USD 12 billion needed for decades to return production toward 3 million barrels per day - likely not before around 2040.

Long-term strategic value, limited near-term impact

Political risk further discourages major oil companies. A history of nationalization, contract revisions, and unresolved arbitration claims has left international producers wary of committing capital without strong legal and fiscal guarantees. While Chevron (CVX) has maintained a limited presence, most large Western firms remain cautious.

Against this backdrop, U.S. interest in Venezuela’s oil appears driven less by immediate market needs and more by long-term strategy. With U.S. shale output expected to plateau later this decade and other Western hemisphere producers peaking in the early 2030s, future supply constraints are becoming a growing concern. Global oil demand continues to rise, and maintaining current production levels will require massive ongoing investment worldwide.

In this context, Venezuela represents a long-duration strategic option rather than a near-term supply shock. Its oil resources could matter in the 2030s if prices rise significantly and political conditions stabilize, but for now, structural limitations explain why markets have largely shrugged off recent developments.

Technical perspective

At present, Brent crude prices are rising dynamically and approaching the area of USD 63.5 per barrel. For now, the USD 59 level has proven to be a fairly strong support zone, with lows in this area recorded in mid-December 2025 as well as earlier in April and May. This support has so far prevented a deeper decline, and with a relatively high probability, prices may continue to move higher in the short term. Technical resistance is located around USD 65-65.5 per barrel, with the next major resistance near USD 70.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Beyond Meat: The Situation Remains Difficult
data.description:
The Beyond Meat investment thesis

Beyond Meat, Inc. (BYND) is a company that has been seen as having a lot of potential in the past. The trend towards healthy eating has brought the meat substitute industry into focus, but unfortunately, progress has stalled somewhat. Now, the main topic regarding Beyond Meat is its massive debt.

Unfortunately, the developments of the last few weeks, as well as the revision of the debt restructuring documents, lead me to keep my Sell rating.

What was my previous coverage like

My last two articles have largely focused on the debt restructuring at Beyond Meat and the fact that this will probably lead to significant dilution. Although debt restructuring was the right move to secure the company's survival in the short term, whether the turnaround will work remains to be seen.

Beyond Meat's mixed shelf offering

On December 23, Beyond Meat issued a mixed shelf offering. A mixed shelf offering is when a company registers securities that can be sold at a later time. The advantage is that it is now possible to issue new shares or take on debt quickly without a lot of paperwork. These shares can now remain on the shelf for up to three years and can be issued whenever the market outlook is positive.

Based on this, one can assume that the dilution will continue, but I think that's something that was expected.

Some observations about the debt restructuring from the end of last year

One point I did not mention before is that the new 2030 loan is secured, and it is a PIK loan. PIK stands for “payment in kind.”

A PIK, or payment in kind, is a type of high-risk loan or bond that allows borrowers to pay interest with additional debt, rather than cash. That makes it an expensive, high-risk financing instrument since the size of the debt may increase quickly, leaving lenders with big losses if the borrower is unable to pay back the loan.

Source: Wikipedia

The above passage would be for a standard PIK loan. But as the PIK at Beyond Meat is a PIK toggle, there is a slight difference.

PIK toggles, also known as "pay if you want," are slightly less risky than PIKs, as borrowers pay interest in cash and may "toggle" to payment in kind at the discretion of the borrower ("pay if you want").

Source: Wikipedia.

This means that with a PIK toggle, it is initially assumed that interest payments will be made in cash and not through the issuance of new debt.

Sometimes, the borrower may also be able to PIK some portion of the interest (usually half) while paying the rest in cash; at times, only some of the interest may be paid in kind and the rest is cash-only.

Source: Wikipedia.

But it also seems possible to pay part of the interest with normal PIK, so by taking on new debt, and the rest with cash. Often, using PIK results in an additional charge of a few basis points, which increases the interest rate. I just wanted to mention these PIK loans because they are rare and easy to overlook.

It will be important to see exactly how the interest payments are made now and at what intervals. This could provide valuable insights for the future.

Strike Price Warrants

At the end of last year, the strike price for Unprocessed Food's warrants was also reduced from $3.26 to $1.95. My assessment of the situation is that a share price of $3.26 is probably unlikely in the near future, which is why the strike price was lowered. The new strike price is also approximately in line with the conversion rate that we will see in the next section.

Conversion rates for senior secured notes due in 2030

Beyond Meat, Inc. (NASDAQ: BYND) (the “Company” or “Beyond Meat”), a leader in plant-based meat, today announced the initial conversion rate for its 7.00% Convertible Senior Secured Second Lien PIK Toggle Notes due 2030 (the “2030 Convertible Notes”) will be 572.7784 shares of the Company’s common stock per $1,000 principal amount of the 2030 Convertible Notes, which represents a conversion price of approximately $1.7459 per share of common stock.

Source: Beyond Meat.

What I found interesting about this announcement is that the conversion price is around $1.7. So, we have a reference point at which it could become interesting, as well as a point that could potentially limit the upside. But I don't think the upside is the main topic at the moment, as the shares are currently trading at $1.03 (4:00 PM, 01/08/26), so it will be interesting to see if the shares fall below the $1 threshold at any point. If a company has an average share price of less than $1 for 30 consecutive days, Nasdaq will issue a deficiency notice. The company then has 180 days to resolve the price deficiency.

Valuation

In my last article, I estimated the cash value per share at $0.125. If we add on top of that the value of the machines and the brand, I would estimate that a fair value is currently around $0.50 to $0.75. However, we must consider that the cash value per share will probably decrease each quarter, as the cash burn is still ongoing. This means that the $0.50 to $0.75 range could also be too high and require significant adjustment in the next quarter.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I would say that the current situation is difficult, as the level of debt restricts the scope for maneuver and makes a turnaround more challenging. However, I don't think a turnaround is impossible. But the risk is still too high for me personally. So I have to stick with my Sell rating.
